summary: Shaw writes an open letter to Slack proposing a simple feature: a paid membership checkbox for Slack teams. He wants to use Slack as a platform to teach coding, where students would pay to join his Slack team, with Slack handling all payment processing and taking a cut. He argues Slack already has every feature needed except the ability to invert who pays, and frames it as a mutual business opportunity that would save him from dealing with payment and membership management overhead.
key_points: Slack could become a paid community platform with one simple feature: charging members to join a team
Content creators shouldn't have to build payment infrastructure when platforms already have it
The proposed model benefits both parties: creator provides content and promotion, platform handles payments and takes a cut
Shaw identifies a gap between Slack's existing B2B model and potential creator-economy use cases
money_quotes: All I want is a checkbox when I create my 'team' that reads: 'Members pay $_____ to join.'
I'd rather just hang out in the Slack and answer questions and have them pay you so you pay me.
Just need the ability to invert who pays and it's nearly perfect.
You literally have every single feature I want to make this work. I just don't want to deal with the bullshit of payments and invites and booting people who don't pay and all that crap.
I have the clout and the skills. You have the platform.
key_insight: Platforms like Slack sit on untapped creator-economy potential that could be unlocked by simply inverting their payment model to let members pay creators through the platform.
